preamble: The shift from the voiced velar fricative /ɣ/ to the palatal glide /j/ is a classic example of palatalization found in many language histories. Before front vowels, /ɣ/ is articulated further forward in the mouth. This fronted variant, a palatal approximant or fricative, naturally merges with the existing phoneme /j/.

examples.note: The Proto-Germanic /ɣ/ became /j/ in Old English after front vowels or in syllable-initial position.
In some dialects, the velar fricative is fronted to a palatal glide.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
